tip: Aim for 7-9 hours of sleep a night, and keep consistent sleep and wake times - even on weekends - to improve sleep quality.
A brisk 30-minute daily walk measurably lowers blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.
Adults should get at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week, plus muscle-strengthening on 2 or more days.
Fill half your plate with vegetables and fruit at meals to increase fiber and lower chronic-disease risk.
Drink water instead of sugary drinks; cutting sugary beverages is one of the simplest ways to reduce excess calories.
Wash your hands with soap for at least 20 seconds to cut the spread of respiratory and diarrheal illness.
Limit added sugars to less than 10% of daily calories to support heart and metabolic health.
Stand up and move for a few minutes every hour; breaking up long sitting periods benefits metabolic health.
Stay up to date on recommended vaccines, including the annual flu vaccine, to prevent serious illness.
Reduce salt intake to under 5 grams (about one teaspoon) per day to help control blood pressure.
Replace refined grains with whole grains like brown rice and whole-wheat bread for more fiber and steadier energy.
If you smoke, quitting at any age improves health; lung function begins to recover within weeks.
Use sunscreen of SPF 15 or higher and seek shade midday to lower skin-cancer risk.
Build strength twice a week; muscle mass supports metabolism, balance, and bone health as you age.
Eat fish or other sources of omega-3 a couple of times a week to support heart health.
Keep alcohol within low-risk limits; less is better, and no amount is the safest option for overall health.
Add beans, lentils, or other legumes to meals for affordable protein and fiber.
Take the stairs when you can; short bursts of activity add up across the day.
Practice good dental hygiene: brush twice daily and floss once to protect gums and teeth.
Manage stress with brief daily breathing or relaxation breaks; chronic stress affects sleep and heart health.
Check food labels and choose products lower in saturated fat, sodium, and added sugar.
Get your blood pressure checked regularly; high blood pressure often has no symptoms.
Snack on a handful of unsalted nuts instead of chips for heart-healthy fats and fiber.
Limit screen time before bed; light exposure can delay sleep onset and reduce sleep quality.
Aim for at least 25-30 grams of fiber a day from vegetables, fruit, whole grains, and legumes.
Warm up before exercise and cool down after to reduce injury risk.
Keep a refillable water bottle nearby; mild dehydration can cause fatigue and poor concentration.
Maintain a healthy weight through balanced eating and regular activity to lower diabetes and heart-disease risk.
Cook at home more often; home-prepared meals tend to be lower in salt, sugar, and unhealthy fat.
Schedule recommended health screenings for your age and risk factors - early detection saves lives.
Choose low-fat or fat-free dairy, or fortified plant alternatives, for calcium without excess saturated fat.
Protect your hearing: lower the volume on headphones and limit time in loud environments.
Move more in daily life - park farther away, walk during calls - to reach activity goals painlessly.
Eat a variety of colored vegetables and fruits to get a broad range of vitamins and antioxidants.
Keep a consistent meal pattern and avoid skipping breakfast to help steady energy and appetite.
If you sit for work, set a reminder to stretch your back, neck, and shoulders periodically.
Get outside for daylight in the morning to help regulate your sleep-wake cycle.
Wash fruits and vegetables before eating to reduce foodborne-illness risk.
Keep moving as you age; regular activity helps prevent falls by improving strength and balance.
Replace sugary breakfast cereals with oatmeal topped with fruit for lasting fullness.
